---
title: "Rung 4 · Simulation validation"
---

[Setup.]{.lead-in} Real covariates whose distribution you want to preserve, and a
list of causal quantities you want to control.

[Move.]{.lead-in} Generate synthetic data that *looks* real but whose truth you
*set*, then check whether your estimators recover it. This is the
[Credence](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v162/parikh22a.html) idea (Parikh et
al., ICML 2022): learn a covariate generator, then inject known effects and bias.

## Can Credence layer in more than one ATE?

Yes — and that was the motivating question for this project. Credence's knobs are
already *functions of covariates*: a heterogeneous effect surface τ(x) and a
confounding/selection function g(x, z), each entering the training loss as an
additive penalty. Layering further parameters — several confounders, multiple
estimands, mediation — is architecturally natural because **every constraint is
just one more additive term**. It takes code, not a config flag.

This project implements the layered-parameter version as *spec-driven forward
simulation* (the full constrained-optimization training is out of scope). A single
`SimulationSpec` carries:

- a **baseline** h(X) fitted to real data (so generated covariates actually drive Y);
- a heterogeneous effect **τ(X)** (constant, linear, or interaction presets);
- a vector of **confounders**, each with its own outcome strength γⱼ and treatment strength ρⱼ;
- an explicit **bias** g(X, Z) that perturbs only the *observed* outcome, leaving the potential-outcome truth clean.

## Several estimands, one dataset

Because the potential outcomes are known, many estimands are exact at once:

| Estimand | Meaning |
|---|---|
| ATE | average over everyone |
| ATT / ATC | average over the treated / controls |
| CATE[subgroup] | average within a covariate stratum |

## The naive analyst drifts as you stack parameters

Watch a plain difference-in-means pull away from the (fixed) truth as confounding
and measurement bias layer on:

| Scenario | true ATE | naive estimate | drift |
|---|---|---|---|
| no confounding | 1.00 | 1.08 | +0.08 |
| heterogeneous effect | 1.02 | 0.91 | −0.11 |
| layered confounding | 1.01 | 1.82 | +0.81 |
| confounding + measurement bias | 0.98 | 2.42 | +1.44 |

The [interactive app](../app.qmd) lets you move τ and the confounding strength and
watch the truth table and the naive drift update live.

[Caveat.]{.lead-in} Forward simulation preserves the covariate *marginal*, not the
full realism of a constrained fit. Treat it as a controlled benchmark, not a
digital twin.

Next: [close the loop →](05-iteration.qmd)
